data.title: Voya Financial: Right On Fundamentals, But The Re-Rating Is Delayed (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Elevator Thesis

Voya Financial (VOYA) is just not a simple re-rating story anymore.

The investment case is no longer the same since my last coverage on November 12, 2025. Voya's fundamentals continue to improve. The market, however, is not yet ready to give a higher multiple to it. This is mainly due to quarterly volatility, especially in the Employee Benefits segment.

Interestingly, Voya is a staggering $1.1 trillion health and wealth platform today, with its Retirement and Investment management businesses. This has led to a more fee-based earnings profile, which has been underpinned by highly defined contribution inflows and accelerating retirement assets.

The FY 25 results somewhat showcase this strength. The adjusted EPS bumped 22% annually to $8.85, driven by robust retirement growth and capital generation.

Let's zoom in on Q4 and assess where the problem is. Adjusted EPS for Q4 clocked in at $1.94, which was below the estimated $2.09. The difference was caused by the increased compensation costs and volatility in medical stop-loss.

Well, these are not structural issues, but they add enough uncertainty to hold back a clean re-rating.

Furthermore, Voya is currently trading at around $72-$73 with 7.8x forward earnings. Such valuation is normally assigned to weaker insurers. It is not for a business that can generate an 18-19% return on equity, with robust cash flow and capital returns.

The gap is an outcome of the inconsistency of overall earnings, and that's what is keeping the valuation in check.

Therefore, I assign VOYA a 'Hold' rating right now.

The long-term story is still intact. A re-rating will require a couple of clean quarters and not just steady growth.

Strong Retirement Engine Still Underappreciated

VOYA's Q4 25 reinforced that the Retirement business is not a weakness.

The quarter's EPS miss was driven by elevated compensation costs and Employee Benefits volatility. Retirement itself is pretty stable and is surely not a contributing factor in the downside.

More to the point, FY 25 showed steady compounding as organic Defined Contribution net inflows were at $28.2 billion. These were backed by continued employer participation and consistent retirement contribution behavior.

These are not like traditional asset management flows since they are built into payroll systems. That causes them to be cyclic and less sensitive to market cycles.

Moreover, stability is more meaningful when scale is kept in consideration. The OneAmerica acquisition contributed about $60 billion of retirement assets during the year. This pushed Voya's total retirement and investment platform to over $1.1 trillion.

At this level, the business is already operating at scale, where efficiency and stability are more important than sheer growth.

Notably, this growth has not been attained at the cost of profitability.

The retirement margins have remained at the high 30%-40% range, which is a positive sign that scale is under control.

Essentially, Retirement is doing what a good-quality pension and retirement platform is supposed to do. The problem with this is that this strength still exists within total earnings, which the market does not fully trust yet.

Hence, Retirement is not getting a higher multiple in the market.

Employee Benefits is the Missing Piece in Consistency

Employee Benefits is where the earnings consistency starts to lose smoothness.

Again, that's visible in Q4. The segment had an operating loss of approximately $10 million, although full-year results were still positive at $152 million.

That contrast is important as it shows that the underlying business is not worsening, but the quarterly path is still bumpy enough to upset consolidated outcomes.

The primary cause of this mismatch comes from medical stop-loss claims. They are simply unpredictable.

In Q4, reserve strengthening added about $37 million. Interestingly, this pressure was enough to weigh on the segment efficiency and the overall earnings sentiment.

In the meantime, management is working. The 24% increment of the January 2026 cohort is a marker of continued pricing discipline.

The flow of pricing changes is slow, and actions and results are still not linked. The market will eventually react when higher rates are maintained without the pressure of retention or adverse selection. Until then, doubt will remain imprinted in the expectations.

Yes, Retirement in itself is powerful enough to sustain an increased multiple. That said, Employee Benefits is too volatile to view that strength in any clear way. This forces the market to consider the inconsistent consolidated earnings.

This near-term dynamic remains unchanged even with the support programs such as the latest TELUS Health partnership. It is a good long-term move that can enhance health outcomes of employees and reduce claims in the long run. However, it won't stabilize the earnings in the coming few quarters.

Capital Return: Strong Base, Limited Upside Driver

Voya continued to generate solid excess capital in FY 25, coming in around $775 million. That amount of cash production shows a high-quality earnings base, which is backed by returns on equity in the high teens.

Notably, this is not stressed capital creation. It is predictable even during a quarter such as Q4, where the EPS is lower than anticipated.

This robustness is flowing into shareholder returns. In early 2026, Voya completed $150 million in buybacks and has committed another $150 million for Q2. These buybacks are highly efficient at around 7.8x forward earnings. Every dollar invested is returned with a double-digit profit through the low valuation.

This forms a decent base. Shareholder returns are steady even in a tight market, along with a 2.5% dividend yield. That is why the stock fails to break materially lower in the face of earnings volatility.

Despite the announced buybacks of $300 million in Q1 and Q2, the stock has not gotten out of its range of $69 to $74. The capital return is still not strong enough to offset the earnings unevenness.

The balance sheet also has a structural signal. Voya has excess capital with RBC ratios at about 413% as opposed to an estimated 375%.

It is a strength, but it also means that there are few avenues of reinvestment in the short term. Consequently, the capital is being returned naturally as opposed to being aggressively utilized in growth programs.

Capital returns are supporting the stock; however, they do not address the clarity of earnings. It is also more of a stabilizer than a re-rating catalyst.

Valuation Discussion

VOYA continues to trade sideways, and that is the clearest signal at the moment.

VOYA has remained mostly in the range of $69-$74. It has tried both ends of that range, yet it has been unable to maintain a breakout. The market is yet to determine a higher multiple, although the earnings are on the rise.

The stock is in the middle of the range of about $72-$73 (as of writing). It is not distressed or in re-rating. A crucial question is whether Voya will be able to provide stability in the quarterly earnings.

This hesitation is supported by an FY26 consensus EPS of $9.57. The growth is consistent but not high enough to change the expectations significantly. The market is not anticipating a downturn but does desire a clear earnings trajectory.

The stock appears cheap at an estimated 7.8x forward earnings. This is particularly true due to a high capital generation and 18-19% ROE. ROE is not, however, being priced in isolation by the market. It is discounting earnings volatility, mainly driven by Employee Benefits. This is why the multiple stays are capped.

Let's assess the scenario framework.

First is the bear case scenario ($60-$65). Employee Benefits remains volatile and pricing measures partially counter the claims pressure; then the earnings will continue to be uneven.

Meanwhile, in the base case of the stock at $72-$75, Retirement pays off and capital returns remain constant. Employee Benefits only enhance gradually, yet they introduce a bit of lumpiness.

Under the bull case ($88-$95), assuming that Employee Benefits stabilize and Retirement compounds, then earnings are predictable once again. At that point, the market ceases to be volatility-conscious and begins to pay a premium on quality.

The consistency of the path is more important than the direction of earnings. The market is ready to embrace growth, but it has yet to come to terms with the imbalanced execution.

Until then, the stock will be range-bound, with a bias to the base case.

Risks to the Thesis and Takeaway

There are a few things that can delay the story from playing out.

First is the timing of Employee Benefits. When the effects of the 24% pricing actions take more time to become visible, then the earnings may remain disproportionate longer than anticipated. It's also going to slow down the improvement due to retention pressure or adverse selection. That would simply keep the stock stuck in its current range for longer.

Then there's market exposure. Retirement and Investment Management are fairly stable, though the fee income remains dependent on equity markets and bond markets. A sudden change in markets may temporarily decelerate the earnings momentum, despite underlying flows remaining constant.

The third one is capital deployment. Voya is generating strong excess capital, but most of it is still going back to shareholders through buybacks. With limited reinvestment opportunities remaining, growth will have to rely more on financial engineering than on actual operational expansion. That basically restricts the extent to which the multiple can move up.

Additionally, the stock may remain range-bound as long as investors continue to concentrate on previous quarterly volatility. Valuation recovery in such a case might lag behind the business.

In general, these risks do not alter the course of the business. They mainly affect the speed of re-rating. The retirement engine is solid, the generation of capital is sound, and long-term structure is maintained.

In the case of current holders, it is not an urgent reason to exit. For new investors, it is prudent to wait till the cleaner quarters, where the earnings will better represent the core business.

data.title: Applied Materials: A Picks And Shovels Play On The AI Chip Revolution
data.description:
Thesis

Applied Materials, Inc. (AMAT) is, in my view, one of the best ways to get exposure to the AI infrastructure buildout without betting on a single horse. The company is situated in the center of the circuit fabrication universe, selling the tools each and every modern fab relies on. My perspective is supported by three arguments: the company's dominant position as materials engineering leader for angstrom-era chips, a financial position that offers both strong margins and ample capital returns to shareholders, and a multiyear tailwind from boosting DRAM and advanced packaging demand.

However, risk is high with this stock, and valuation is a true debate at current levels. Applied Materials has significant upsides only if current demand for AI driven equipment is sustained; if the cycle drags or if policy headwinds intensify, excess margin for error will remain absent. For buy-and-hold investors willing to tolerate near-term policy knives and the cyclical semiconductor industry, I give Applied Materials a cautious buy score.

Company Overview

Applied Materials was formed in 1967 as one of the first companies built specifically to design and market manufacturing equipment to chipmakers. Today it is the world’s largest supplier of equipment to manufacture semiconductor wafers. Headquartered in Santa Clara, California, its product range includes systems for deposition, etch, inspection, and process control, alongside services and software that enable chip and advanced display production.

In the real world, if a foundry or memory maker is looking to scale down transistors, stack memory layers, or wrap nanosheets in exotic metal gates, Applied Materials is probably the company providing a key piece of that puzzle. It has three reporting segments (Semiconductor Systems, Applied Global Services, and Display), but the Semiconductor Division is clearly the growth driver in the modern era, particularly as AI and GPU computing equipment require ever more complex chip designs.

Qualitative and Quantitative Analysis

What surprises me most about Applied is the quality of its business model. Over the last two years, Applied has averaged an operating margin of about 29%, making it one of the more efficient companies in the semiconductor equipment space. This is not accidental; it speaks to the years of process know-how and strong customer relationships that produce significant switching costs. Once a fab concludes a deposition or etch tool into a high-volume manufacturing line, the removal of the system is prohibitively costly and dangerous.

Free cash flow is telling a similar story. The trailing two-year average free cash flow margin is around 22%, providing much-needed flexibility. It allows management to spend heavily on R&D, a necessity in a business where the physics changes with each new node, but also to reward shareholders. Over the last ten years, Applied Materials has returned almost 90% of its cumulative free cash flow via buybacks and dividends.

Dividend per share has compounded at around 18% a year during this time, and the last 15% increase, to $0.53 a quarter, extends an unbroken nine-year run of increases. I take that as a sign of genuine belief in the sustainability of the cash generation engine.

Finally, the ROIC (return on invested capital) figure is clearly the most illuminating. Applied Materials' five-year average ROIC is approximately 47%, an incredible rate suggesting that the management team always has better than average projects to invest in. Considering the industry is so cyclical, maintaining that type of capital efficiency from one cycle to the next is truly remarkable.

The near‑term revenue picture is dominated by AI‑driven memory capex. Fiscal Q1 2026 was the first quarter to deliver a new record for DRAM equipment—$1.75 billion of revenue—and the company has stated publicly that it expects DRAM to be its fastest-growing segment for calendar 2026. Wolfe Research sees Applied Materials’ DRAM revenue rising around 34% in calendar year 2027—driven by the company’s solid deposition and conductor etch positioning. NAND recovery, though, is less than half those levels - roughly 10% for the same year - pointing towards a more constrained competitive standing at this end of the industry.

Consensus analyst estimates show revenue of roughly $32.5 billion and earnings of roughly $9.2 billion by FY28, with a few more bullish analysts forecasting as much as $35 billion in revenue and $9.8 billion in earnings over the same time horizon. These estimates are aggressive, but not completely inconsistent with the ongoing AI-driven equipment upcycle I observe. I anchor my own valuation to the low end of the consensus EPS band to account for the cyclical nature of the business and inherent capex-reduction risk.

Key Competitors

The three competitors I pay the closest attention to are Lam Research, ASML, and Tokyo Electron.

In terms of deposition and etch, Lam Research (LRCX) is the main competitor, especially in NAND, where it holds a larger share. In terms of EUV lithography, ASML's (ASML) monopoly position is so unique that it is not a straight head-to-head fight, more of a natural complement, but ASML's share of every advanced fab dollar is a restraint on what Applied Materials can capture. Tokyo Electron (TOELY) is the third member of this competitive triumvirate with broad-line equipment offerings, including some particular wins with coater developer tools and some etch apps.

Applied Materials differential advantage over all three is the size of its portfolio and the depth of its materials engineering expertise, which will be more and more critical as the transistor architecture becomes more three-dimensional and multi-material at the angstrom scale. While Lam and Tokyo Electron dominate some of the modules, and ASML is a fixture at the lithography layer, Applied Materials is increasingly at the focal point of the fab’s “materials stack”.

Catalysts and Recent News

The biggest recent catalyst came on April 8 when Applied Materials announced two new fabrication systems built specifically for 2-nanometer and sub-2-nm gate all-around (GAA) transistors. The newly introduced Precision Selective Nitride PECVD platform deposits a protective silicon nitride layer that maintains shallow trench isolation integrity and improves performance per watt through its reduction of parasitic capacitance. Likewise, the Trillium ALD system encloses silicon nanosheets with complex metal gate stacks, allowing chipmakers to tailor transistor performance for various AI workloads.

These already manage the process of material deposition down to individual atom-scale levels, exactly at the point the angstrom era demands. The stock jumped about 9% on the announcement day, and the 30-day return before mid-April was over 18%, indicating the market had expected good news.

On the analyst side, the picture is mixed. Wolfe Research, for example, has raised its price target on the company to $500 from $450 based on the DRAM opportunity and has rated the stock an Outperform. MoffettNathanson , on the other hand, lowered its target on the company to $214 from $231, mainly due to compressed valuation multiples, but still maintains a Buy rating. This $214.00 to $500.00 spread really shows how divided the street is on this name. Wolf’s target is high up in the bullish section of the camp, and MoffettNathanson is more conservative on the company.

Other positives: a new third-party logistics alliance using UPS’s Taiwan hub to distribute in Asia, new U.S. government support for U.S. supply chains with the CHIPS Act, and the aforementioned dividend hike. Negatives: Ongoing Chinese export-control uncertainty is the main overhang. Unspecific management guidance stated that a large % of g/u orders & revenue come from the China exposed fabs. Further tightening of US export controls or China's retaliatory actions could substantially impact the business and margins.

Valuation

That's where the disagreement is between the bulls and bears. On the one hand, Applied trades at about 33 times forward earnings, with the stock recently trading around $400. The consensus average analyst price target is about $435, suggesting only a low to mid single-digit percentage upside - implied certainly not what one would call mind-boggling for a cyclical growth stock.

One of the more popular discounted cash flow models, which assumes the long-term growth and margin component not to be particularly aggressive, suggests a fair value of about $190, implying a significant overvaluation of the stock. I think this model uses far too conservative assumptions regarding long-term trends and undervalues the structural secular trend toward higher IT CapEx and the AI-driven data center infrastructure buildout, as well as the robustness of Applied Materials’ competitive moat. I think the combination of sustained AI-CapEx-driven strength and the company's deep-quality earnings profile supports a premium to the more conservative DCF estimates even if the stock isn't cheap today.

My framework takes the consensus 2028 earnings estimate, which is $9.2 billion (adjusted EPS), and applies a 25-28 times earnings multiple, which seems reasonable given the high ROIC capital goods compounder in a structurally growing semiconductor ecosystem, and discounts back at 10%, representing the current elevated cost of capital and the cyclical risk of the business. That math yields a fair value range of approximately $375-$420 per share, implying that the stock is about fairly valued today and not cheap or expensive. Certainly, a bull case above $450 requires either more than expected DRAM and advanced packaging growth, or some degree of multiple expansion, both of which seem likely but are by no means certain

Risks

Three risks could alter my analysis. One would be a significant tightening of China export restrictions, or a retaliatory move that would significantly erode revenue and reduce margins. A second issue would be a slowing of overall semiconductor capex, a risk that is intrinsic in a cyclical industry that would disproportionately impact equipment vendors and reduce multiples. A third risk is customer concentration: large foundry and memory customers’ purchasing behavior creates lumpiness in orders and additional earnings volatility. A significant increase in any of these three factors would cause me to reassess the thesis.

Conclusion

Applied Materials is a best-in-class business riding a major secular tailwind. Its margins, cash flow conversion, and ROIC are all best in class, and the latest angstrom era tool launches cement its technological leadership at exactly the time when chipmakers need that expertise the most. The valuation is not cheap on most traditional measures, but I think the quality of the franchise and the multi-year AI-driven equipment upcycle mean the premium is warranted. However, the valuation already assumes a good deal of optimism; there is little margin for error should the AI capex cycle temper or policy catalysts turn sour.

For multi-year-horizon investors comfortable with semiconductor cyclicality and who have the stomach for policy noise about China, Applied Materials stays a core holding.

data.title: ATI Inc.: Why I Remain Bullish On Aerospace And Defense Growth
data.description:
In January, I marked ATI Inc. (ATI) a strong buy with a $163.13 bullish price target. Since then, the stock has reached my price target. That may be somewhat remarkable given the market turmoil over the past weeks and aerospace and defense names definitely not being favored by the market. With the price targets reached and earnings coming up later this month, it is a good moment to revisit ATI Inc.

Potential Risks For ATI With Offsets

The increase in oil prices is currently driving up energy prices and inflation. I believe that as a critical provider of material alloys such as titanium alloys, nickel-based alloys, superalloys, and forged components, ATI has a strong position to pass through costs using raw material surcharges and escalation clauses. The business is highly energy intensive, but those can also be passed through price resets and broader escalation clauses. So, the impact of higher raw material costs, energy costs, and inflation can generally be passed through with some lag, causing a temporary margin contraction. However, the cost side of the business should be shielded extremely well also because businesses like ATI operate under long-term agreements with their suppliers.

On the demand side, there are some risks. ATI generates 70% of its sales from aerospace and defense and 30% from other industries. Those industries already had quite a difficult time, and with economic growth under pressure, the revenues could be under pressure. The stock market has reached new highs, but it seems to completely ignore that GDP growth outlooks have weakened. That is an obvious risk, because while the stock market ignores it, a reduction in the growth outlook may result in lower growth in ATI’s end markets. In aerospace and defense, I do believe that defense demand will remain stable. In Aerospace, I do not expect sales to original equipment manufacturers to be affected for the simple reason that we have not seen any tweaks to airplane production rates. There is, however, a risk that we should keep in mind, and that is that airlines have started flying less, and that also may result in lower demand for commercial MRO. As utilization decreases or active fleets shrink, demand for parts and services also declines unless airlines opportunistically pull forward maintenance events.

Overall, I do believe that while there may be some soft spots even in aerospace and defense, there should be substantial growth ahead.

Analysts Expect Modest Revenue Growth With Strong EPS Growth

For the first quarter, analysts are expecting ATI to report $1.19 billion in revenues, marking 3.8% sales growth. We note that over the past six months, the estimates have come down by 2.5%, and by 1.8% over the past three months, with a 0.4% increase over the past month. So, there is no expected major impact due to the war, and that shows the resilience of ATI. A growth of 3.8% does not sound impressive, but we note that aerospace and defense companies tend to realize their growth from a lower base driven by end-of-year increases in deliveries of products, parts, and materials, leaving the subsequent quarter lower on deliverable inventories.

Earnings per share are estimated to be $0.88, marking 22.3% growth, with estimates having increased 2.3% over the past six months, 3.2% over the past three months, and 0.9% over the past month. So, we are also not seeing any impact on earnings per share from potential higher costs. The company will report Q1 2026 earnings on the 30th of April, and it will be interesting to see whether they have any comments on demand and the cost side of the business, but it is looking a lot like ATI is well-positioned to absorb any costs, and demand remains robust.

When it comes to beating analyst estimates, we note that the company’s track record on revenues is not the best, with six misses over the past 12 quarters, including a miss in the prior quarter. On earnings per share, the track record is more robust with one miss over the past twelve quarters, and the last reading that missed estimates was in Q3 2024.

ATI Margins And Free Cash Flow Drive Upside

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

We note that ATI is not cheap at current prices since it trades at 28x EV/EBITDA, and the company has been trading at twice its median trading multiple. However, it seems that ATI has made a step change from trading like a material cyclical name to a cyclical aerospace and defense name. It is still cyclical, but it is substantially better to be valued as an aerospace and defense company. Given the high exposure to aerospace and defense, I do believe it makes sense to be valued in line with the aerospace peers, and that would indicate a $200.83 price target with 22% upside, which is enough to maintain my strong buy rating. We also note that the company’s price level suggests that it now has a tendency to trade in line with peers.

For ATI, we see that the operating leverage has been kicking in, and as a result, cash flows are sharply improving. This looks like one of the best operating leverage plays I have come across in the industry. Capital intensity is set to moderate in the years ahead and fall to 4% of revenues by 2028. I do expect the company to maintain a sizable share repurchase program. In terms of net debt leverage, we note that the company is in an extremely strong position, and that may also mean that it will refinance some of its net debt maturities since it does not really need to bring the leverage down. I have modeled that they will indeed repay the maturities in 2027, which are the biggest block of debt obligations in the coming years, and it shows that even then the company should be able to maintain a cash position from which it will rebound to around $350 million in the year after.

Conclusion: ATI Operating Leverage Strength Shines

I believe that ATI is well positioned to offset any increase in cost it may experience due to cost inflation and higher raw material costs, which in turn may be padded through long-term supply agreements. In terms of demand, I also do not foresee a major demand shake-up, and in defense, we may even see some incremental upside as utilization of military aircraft has increased. As a result, I am maintaining my strong buy rating.

data.title: Ardagh Metal Packaging: Execution Holds, Tailwinds Build, Valuation Undemanding
data.description:
After three years without a rating, we are back to comment on and analyze Ardagh Metal Packaging (AMBP). The company is a global leader in beverage cans and a best-in-class specialty mix. Its mother company, Ardagh Group, performed a recapitalization on 12 November 2025, which included the mandatory exchange of all outstanding senior unsecured bonds for partial equity in a newly restructured entity. This will likely prevent any further negative press for Ardagh Metal Packaging, which is a separate, ring-fenced entity.

Why are we positive?

Our decision to take a follow-up note was prompted by discussions at the Euronext STAR Conference, where Zignago Vetro’s IR highlighted persistently elevated energy costs across the sector. This was due to the escalation in the Middle East. While the bear case may frame this as another transition year, we believe the company continues to execute effectively, as evidenced by a further EBITDA beat in Q4 and initial Q1 guidance slightly ahead of Wall Street consensus. To add numbers, trends were positive in 4Q, but the company reported solid adjusted EBITDA of $166 million, above the Street average of $157 million, and adjusted EPS of $0.03, above the Street average of $0.02.

Fig. 1

Ardagh Metal Packaging is exposed to changes in energy prices. However, we report that the company's annual report states that it has "an active hedging strategy to fix a significant proportion of our energy costs through contractual arrangements directly with our suppliers. Our policy is to purchase natural gas and electricity by entering into forward price-fixing arrangements with suppliers for the majority of our anticipated requirements for the year ahead and for further diminishing portions of our anticipated requirements for subsequent years." The company was heavily impacted by the supply shock caused by the Russia-Ukraine war. While there is no disclosure on energy hedge in Ardagh Metal Packaging's slide, particularly compared with the transparency typically seen in the aviation sector (International Consolidated Airlines Group at 62% and Deutsche Lufthansa AG at 77%), here at the Lab, we believe the company has experienced past volatility and maintains an active and significant protection level.

The company expects volume to grow in LatAm and the EU; however, it reports softer volume contracts in NA. Last year, the company still outperformed the industry. However, Ardagh's story could become more interesting as we head into 2027, with new contract wins. We believe this sub-segment will grow faster than population growth. This is because: 1) the use of metal, which is fully recyclable, reduces the need for virgin raw materials, and 2) the use of metal enables energy savings based on limited raw material extraction and lower melting furnaces' energy consumption. Here at the Lab, we believe this is not well displayed in the company's communication. Megatrends and ESG support Ardagh Metal Packaging's demand. This is also combined with earnings stability, given a pass-through provision on input costs. We well understood that in the Zignago Vetro meeting.

Looking at Q4 FCF, the company delivered $172 million, compared to a Wall Street consensus expectation of $254 million. Net debt/LTM EBITDA is now at ~5.3x. This is a high number. However, the company remains with a green bond in 2025. This included the redemption of the preferred shares, so there will be a benefit from paying a lower dividend in 2026, which a higher cash interest payment will slightly offset. The net benefit is $10 million annually. The company maintains a yearly dividend of $0.4/share (paid quarterly). In 2025, Ardagh's dividend payment was $262 million and was not matched by its FCF generation, which reached €232 million, as shown in Fig. . 2.

Bears might raise questions about Ardagh Metal Packaging's dividend sustainability. However, the company has reiterated its commitment to its dividend policy. At the Lab, we believe the company may reduce its payout (given a dividend yield of almost 10%), but a reiteration of the payment suggests they can sustain this commitment. A reduction might also support a deleveraging process (given a net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 5.3x). Having said that, we can value the company using either a dividend growth model or an FCF yield; in either case, the result is similar to 10%. This means a buy rating.

Fig. 2

Lastly, on a positive note, the company was subject to heavy short interest in the past. In late 2025, a few well-known hedge fund managers entered Ardagh. In particular, Arini Capital Management initiated a new position of 11.25 million shares. This was followed by Monarch Alternative Capital, which also added 5.35 million shares.

Valuation

Looking ahead, the company guided 2026 adjusted EBITDA to $750–$775 million, with Q1 adjusted EBITDA of $160–$170 million (Fig. . 2). The company reported solid results from North America in the quarter. In 2026, we expect volume/mix in Brazil to be up low-to-mid-single digits, with North America declining (despite positive Q1 evolution) due to contract resets and footprint changes. Europe's growth will likely grow at 3%. Here at the Lab, we are also positive this year, given the football World Cup, which might provide a positive tailwind. Even applying the low end of the company's EBITDA guidance of $750 million, with a 5-year historical EV/EBITDA multiple of 9.5x, this would result in an enterprise value of $7.12 billion. Considering the debt ($3.9 billion), there is a 32% upside to the current estimates. This is also combined with a 10% dividend or a deleverage path of the same amount. Therefore, we reinstated Ardagh Metal Packaging's rating to buy and set a target price of $5.6 per share.

Risks

The company's key risks include supply/demand imbalance and higher energy costs. Additionally, Ardagh Metal Packaging might be impacted by higher maintenance CAPEX. The company carries a net leverage of 5.3x. Equity is highly sensitive to small changes in enterprise value and the interest-rate environment. Dividend sustainability is another risk because it was not supported by FCF generation. Any operational weakness could force a cut, which would likely cause a sharp sell-off in the stock and cause a multiple de-rating. As a controlled company (76% owned by Ardagh Group), the company has limited rights and recourse for minority shareholders. While secular trends are favorable, a recession could impact volumes.

Conclusion

Overall, this is a well-run company with supportive trends ahead, solid shareholder remuneration, and a not-pricey valuation. Hedge fund sentiment seems to revert, and this is usually the first factor to swing sell-side optimism in Wall Street equity research in the medium term. We reinitiated the company with a buy rating.

data.title: BDCs That Put Their Money Where Their Mouth Is
data.description:
The BDC sector (BIZD) has landed on the chopping block of many bears.

Given the intensity at which bearish headlines get pumped across the financial media channels, we are most likely well aware of the "so-called" issues:

  • Many hidden cockroaches such as Tricolor and First Brands.
  • Huge exposure to SaaS, which is likely to struggle due to the advancing AI.
  • Exodus of capital from private BDCs and the corresponding liquidity caps.
  • Opaque valuation methods.

There are many more, but these are probably the main ones.

Most of these risks started to emerge from mid-January this year:

On a YTD basis, the index is down by ~7%, which, considering the pre-existing discounts, has contributed to a sector median discount of 27%. The market-cap weighted index (RJ BDC Index) is down ~11%.

In the meantime, almost all BDC managers have characterized this dynamic as a significant disconnect from the underlying reality or the underlying financial situation. This is at least how I interpret their comments in Q4 2025 earnings calls and in some select conferences.

Personally, I also see the situation in a similar way - i.e., a huge buying opportunity. You can find more details in my recent piece here: BDCs: The Time To Buy This 14% Yielding Sector Is Now

Now, given the combination of a) deep-value level discounts and b) strong conviction about the disconnect between value and share price, it would be only logical if we saw heavy insider buying activity.

Notable insider buying activity would, in my view, put a cherry on top of a specific (discounted) BDC's investment case.

Conversely, if there were no insider buying involved, especially in the case of significantly lower share price and deep discount, I would probably question the validity of BDCs' investment cases. I'm not saying that I would sell, but at least I would reexamine the position and perhaps trim the exposure a bit.

Without further ado, let's take a look at 20 BDCs and their insider activity.

The analysis

There are a couple of aspects I would like to underscore before you see the table that I have developed.

Ticker: 20 BDCs that, in my view, are well-known.

Acquisitions: Net buying activity of the selected BDCs over the past 3-month period (Jan 15 - April 15). Note, from all 20 BDCs, there was only 1 case in which some selling was done: BlackRock TCP Capital Corp (TCPC).

Insiders: The number of unique insiders (persons) who have made the purchases.

Average: Acquisition amount divided by number of insiders.

3M price: BDC's share price movement in the past 3-month period.

Ticker Acquisitions, $ Insiders, # Average, $ 3M price, %
TSLX 10.8M 6 1.8M -15%
HTGC 2.8M 11 255K -17.3%
ARCC 943K 4 236K -9.6%
TRIN 554K 3 185K -3.6%
MSDL 340K 4 85K -9.1%
TCPC 237K 4 59K -25.6%
PFLT 172K 2 86K -10.3%
KBDC 138K 1 138K -3.8%
OBDC 113K 1 113K -8.6%
FSK 75K 2 37K -24.1%
CSWC 73K 2 36K 1.1%
BXSL 67K 3 22K -6.1%
MAIN - - - -9.9%
FDUS - - - -7.3%
CION - - - -21.2%
CCAP - - - -9.7%
GAIN - - - -10.0%
GLAD - - - 8.3%
TPVG - - - -20.3%
HRZN - - - -29.6%

P.S. Insider trading data is gathered from SecForm4.

Key takeaways

From the analysis or data points above, I think there are two key takeaways - other than what is very obvious from the table, like the fact that the situation for Sixth Street Specialty (TSLX) is extremely bullish - which we can extract and factor into our investment decision making.

1. The purchases for really heavily discounted BDCs have been rare.

To be honest, what has surprised me is the fact that many of the most discounted vehicles have not been subject to at least above average insider buying. In fact, most of them have not seen any insider buying at all.

For example, Horizon Technology Finance (HRZN), TriplePoint Venture Growth (TPVG) and CION Investment Corp (CION) trade at P/NAV of 0.65x, 0.59x and 0.52x, respectively, all well below the sector median of 0.73x. These BDCs are also among the ones that have experienced the biggest share price drops in the past 3 months. For FSK KKR Capital Corp. (FSK), the story is quite similar, although there has been a tiny bit of purchasing made.

The way we can read this is that the insiders seeing the opportunity to buy their businesses almost at 50 cents on the dollar have still decided, for some reason, to sit on the sidelines. It is a bit speculative, but one potential explanation could be related to the expectation of a further sell-off, which could potentially be followed right after the publication of Q1 2026 reports.

2. The purchases have been notable for discounted internal BDCs.

Most of my closest followers who are interested in BDCs are probably already aware of my bullish stance on internally managed BDCs.

In the table I have included all of them: Trinity Capital (TRIN), Capital Southwest (CSWC), Main Street Capital (MAIN) and Hercules Capital (HTGC).

We can see that neither MAIN nor CSWC have made significant purchases (MAIN's insiders have not bought at all). I think that we should not take this as a big negative. Two reasons:

  1. MAIN and CSWC are both the most expensive BDCs in the sector with P/NAV of 1.60x and 1.35x, respectively.
  2. Their share prices have not experienced a violent correction.

What this, however, implies is that the upside potential from the price gains perspective is likely limited here (due to no deep discounts), which is completely fine with me. The main criteria for me are a) durable income and b) resilient NAV, and then only comes the "c"), which is decent price performance.

For TRIN and HTGC the insider buying activity has been significant.

Let me quickly unpack this.

TRIN's case is a bit tricky.

On the one hand, the share price has not moved that much in the past 3-month period. Plus, the current P/NAV is 1.12x, which is materially above the sector median.

On the other hand, we have to appreciate the fact that TRIN is heavily discounted on a relative basis. Namely, internally traded BDCs MAIN, CSWC and HTGC trade at much higher multiples (see above for MAIN and CSWC; HTGC trades at P/NAV of 1.22x).

At the same time TRIN has below-average SaaS exposure, below-average debt levels, below-average PIK and non-accruals, and, importantly, growing NII per share Q4 statistics, which was not the case for other peers (see more details on TRIN in my article here). So, it is only logical that the BDC insiders welcomed the slightly decreased share price and reacted accordingly.

HTGC is a more straightforward case.

The most likely reason why the insiders have really loaded up on HTGC shares is related to the ~17% sell-off, which has been comparable to that of TPVG, HRZN and FSK - all BDCs with surging non-accruals, dividend cuts, etc.

The only rational explanation for this is HTGC's massive exposure to SaaS names. Apart from this, the key fundamentals remain in rock-solid shape (similar to TRIN) with, for example, non-accruals at 0.2% of portfolio cost, debt-to-equity of 1.03x, portfolio company interest coverage of 4.9x and base dividend coverage level of 120%. All these metrics are significantly better than for the sector average player (and, more importantly, very robust on an absolute level). See more details on HTGC's case in my ticker-specific article here.

So it is only logical that if the management thinks that the SaaS-driven selloff is detached from reality, then they would be heavy buyers of the ~17% dip opportunity.

Final remarks

The overall BDC insider transaction activity sends very bullish signals. The fact that none of my selected 20 BDCs have been subject to net selling activity speaks volumes.

However, I would be careful about BDCs that trade at steep discounts (close to 40% to 50% below NAV) but have not been scooped up by their insiders. FSK, HRZN, TPVG and CION are the main ones.

Finally, it is encouraging to see bullish confirmation signs on TSLX, TRIN, HTGC, Ares Capital (ARCC) and even Morgan Stanley Direct Lending (MSDL), on which I have active buy ratings.

In a nutshell, this is another structural data point that argues in favor of BDCs and supports my strategic recommendation to go big on BDCs.

data.description:
“Trump Says Hormuz Strait Is Fully Open, Cites Iran.” “Hormuz Passage ‘Completely Open’ to Commercial Ships During Ceasefire: Iran FM.” “Trump Says Iran Will Suspend Nuclear Program as Hormuz Reopens.”

April 17 – AFP: “US President Donald Trump said Friday that Iran had agreed to never again shut the Strait of Hormuz, during a string of social media posts indicating a peace deal with Tehran was near. ‘Iran has agreed to never close the Strait of Hormuz again. It will no longer be used as a weapon against the World,’ Trump said on his Truth Social network…”

April 17 – Bloomberg (Patrick Sykes): “Passage through the Strait of Hormuz ‘is not possible without the coordination of the Islamic Republic Guard Corps navy,’ Iranian state TV cites a senior military official as saying. Passage of military vessels through the strait remains forbidden. Ships can only pass via the route approved by the Ports and Maritime Organization of Iran.”

“Iran to Close Strait of Hormuz if US Blockade Persists: Fars.” “Iran Hardliners Attack Araghchi’s Hormuz Tweet as ‘Incomplete and Misleading’.” “Iran TV: Ships Passing Through Hormuz Need IRGC Coordination.” “Iran’s Revolutionary Guard Says It Will Decide Who Crosses Hormuz: WSJ.” “Iran Considers a Naval Blockade to be a Violation of the Ceasefire.” “Iran to Take Action if US Blockade Persists: Foreign Ministry.” “Iran Tells Mediators Will Limit Ships Crossing Straight: WSJ” “Iran Tells Mediators Will Charge Tolls During Ceasefire: WSJ” “‘Ships Are Moving’ Through Hormuz, Lutnick Says: WSJ” “Hormuz Remains ‘Effectively Closed’ Despite Iran’s Pledge, Kpler Says.”

April 17 – Bloomberg (Alexander Pearson): “Asked if Iran agreed to stop enriching uranium, President Trump said ‘yes,’ according to an X post by a NewsNation reporter… ‘They agreed to everything,’ the reporter quotes Trump as adding.”

“Trump Says Iranians Have ‘Agreed to Everything,’ Including Removal of Enriched Uranium.” “Trump Says Iran Has Agreed to Nearly All His Demands.” “Trump: ‘No Sticking Points’ Remain With Iran, Iran to Stop Backing Proxies.” “Trump: ‘Don’t Think There’s Too Many Significant Differences’ With Iran.”

“The U.S.A. will get all Nuclear ‘Dust,’ created by our great B2 Bombers - No money will exchange hands in any way, shape, or form. This deal is in no way subject to Lebanon, either, but the USA will, separately, work with Lebanon, and deal with the Hezbollah situation in an appropriate manner. Israel will not be bombing Lebanon any longer. They are PROHIBITED from doing so by the U.S.A. Enough is enough!!! Thank you! President DJT.”

“Trump Says U.S. Will Work With Iran to Remove Enriched Uranium.” “U.S. Proposal Would Allow Iran to Access $20 Billion for Handing Over Enriched Uranium.” “‘No Money is Changing Hands’: Trump Rejects $20 billion Iran ‘Nuclear Dust’ Deal Claim.” “Trump Says US Will Not Release Frozen Iran Funds.”

April 17 – Bloomberg (Andrea Salcedo): “President Trump says the US will recover ‘nuclear dust’ from Iran ‘very soon,’ Reuters reports, citing a call with the president in which he discussed Iran’s enriched uranium… The administration will also bring Iran’s uranium to the US, Trump tells Reuters.”

April 17 – AFP: “President Donald Trump said Friday that the United States and Iran would jointly remove uranium from Tehran’s nuclear sites with excavators under any peace deal, before the material is transferred to US territory… ‘Somebody said, how are we going to get the nuclear dust? We’re going to get it by going in with Iran, with lots of excavators,’ Trump told a gathering of the conservative Turning Point USA movement... ‘We need the biggest excavators you can imagine,’ he added. ‘But we’re going to go in together with Iran. We’re going to get it. We’re going to take it back home to the USA very soon.’”

“Trump Boasts Iran ‘Agreed To Everything’ — Iran Quickly Says It Won’t Give Up Uranium.” “Iran Rejects US President Trump’s Uranium Transfer Claim.” “Iran Rejects Trump Claim on Deal to Surrender Nuclear Material Stockpiles.”

April 17 – Al Jazeera: “Iran’s Foreign Ministry spokesperson Esmaeil Baghaei dismisses Trump’s claim that Tehran has agreed to allow its enriched uranium to be moved to the US. ‘Enriched uranium is as sacred to us as Iranian soil and will not be transferred anywhere under any circumstances,’ Baghaei was quoted as saying by Tasnim news agency.”

April 17 – AFP: “Iran’s foreign ministry on Friday said the country’s stockpile of enriched uranium would not be transferred ‘anywhere’, denying an earlier claim by US President Donald Trump that the Islamic republic had agreed to hand it over. ‘Iran’s enriched uranium is not going to be transferred anywhere,’ Iranian foreign ministry spokesman Esmaeil Baqaei told state TV. ‘Transfer of Iran’s enriched uranium to the US has never been raised in negotiations.’”

“Trump Says Most Main Points ‘Finalized’ in Talks Toward Deal.” “Trump Says Iran Agreed Unlimited Suspension of Nuclear Program.” “Trump Tells Axios He Expects Iran Deal ‘In a Day or Two.’” “Iran’s Baghaei Says US Statements ‘Filled with Contradictions.’”

April 17 – Al Jazeera: “Iran’s Foreign Ministry spokesperson Esmaeil Baghaei has said remarks by US officials regarding the Strait of Hormuz have been contradictory, in a sign of ‘desperation and helplessness’. ‘We should not be influenced by the other side’s tweets,’ the spokesperson said. ‘The statements by American officials are filled with contradictions and lies, and this is nothing new.’”

April 17 – Al Jazeera: “A Pakistani source has told Al Jazeera that much more needs to be done in talks between Iran and the US before a lasting peace in the region. An Iranian source said that the next steps between the US and Iran will involve agreeing on a framework for negotiations – not a final agreement.”

Friday evening tweets from Speaker of the Parliament – and head of Iran’s ceasefire talks delegation in Islamabad - Mohammad-Bagher Ghalibaf (translated from Persian to English by Google). 1- The US President made seven claims in one hour, all of which are false. 2- They did not win the war with these lies and they will definitely not get anywhere in negotiations. 3- As the blockade continues, the Strait of Hormuz will not remain open. 4- Transit through the Strait of Hormuz will be carried out based on the ‘designated route’ and with ‘Iran’s permission.'5- The open or closedness of the Strait and the regulations governing it are determined by the field, not social networks. 6- Media warfare and public opinion engineering are an important part of the war, and the Iranian nation will not be affected by these tricks. Read the real and accurate news of the negotiations in the recent interview of the Foreign Ministry spokesman.”

And, finally, late Friday night from the Wall Street Journal (Hannah Miao): “President Trump said he may not extend the ceasefire with Iran if no deal is reached by Wednesday, when the current pause in fighting is set to expire. ‘Maybe I won’t extend it. So you have the blockade, and unfortunately we’ll have to start dropping bombs again,’ Trump told reporters… on Air Force One.”

Teetering on the edge of a deal with Iran to end the war — or a deeper mess. While hopeful, all the bedlam has me leaning toward the latter. Markets are all in on the former, rendering the “markets hate uncertainty” maxim pathetically archaic. These days, uncertainty and mayhem are relished. The President is spooking the entire world – just not stock markets.

“Rally Drives Nasdaq 100 to Longest Winning Streak Since 2013.” All-time highs for the S&P 500, Nasdaq Composite, Nasdaq 100, S&P 400 Mid-Caps, small-cap Russell 2000, NYSE Securities Broker/Dealers, Dow Transports, and the Value Line Arithmetic index (for starters).

The S&P 500’s 4.5% gain this week boosted the rally off March 30th lows (13 sessions) to 13.0%. The Nasdaq 100 jumped 6.2%, with the rally off lows at 17.0%. The Mag7 Index surged 8.5% this week, boosting m-t-d gains to 14.5%. The semiconductors surged another 7.5% this week, boosting y-t-d gains to 34.9%. The Transports surged 10.2%, with 2026 gains up to 29.2%. The Broker/Dealers jumped 7.5% for the week.

The Goldman Sachs Most Short Index spiked 13.8% higher this week, with the rally off March 30th lows now at a spiffy 28.5%. How many extraordinary squeezes have we witnessed over recent years?

While stocks have recovered all war losses (plus some), bonds have been much more circumspect. Inflation. Yields were down this week, with 10-year Treasury yields seven bps lower. But 10-year yields remain 31 bps higher in the seven weeks of war. Two-year yields (down 9bps this week) remain 33 bps higher, five-year yields (down 10bps) 34 bps higher, and long-bond yields (2 lower) 27 bps higher. While down 13 bps this week, MBS yields have jumped 32 bps since the war’s onset.

Key global bond markets struggled to muster much of a rally. UK 10-year gilt yields (down 7bps) have jumped 53 bps since the war’s outbreak to 4.76%, Italian yields (down 17bps) 41 bps to 3.68%, and Greek yields (down 15bps) 38 bps to 3.65%. Retreating only a basis point this week to 2.42%, Japanese 10-year JGB yields remain 30 bps higher. French yields (3.58%) are 36 bps higher, German yields (3.06%) 32 bps, and Spanish yields (3.39%) 33 bps higher.

From crazy speculative market trading action to booming Q1 financial sector results, bubble confirmation was rather conspicuous this week.

April 14 – Financial Times (Eric Platt): “BlackRock drew in $130bn of capital in the first quarter… Inflows in the quarter were driven by its iShares exchange traded funds business, with chief executive Larry Fink characterising the results as ‘one of the strongest starts to a year in BlackRock’s history’. ‘Our model is working, and we’re more confident than ever in the opportunity we see ahead for our firm,’ he said. BlackRock’s profits increased 46% from the previous year to $2.2bn as revenues jumped more than a quarter to $6.7bn… The $130bn the company collected included $72bn across its equity funds and $34bn to its fixed income vehicles in the quarter…”

The major banks “crushed” Q1 earnings, as equities trading records were posted across Goldman, JPMorgan, Bank of America, and Citigroup. JPMorgan revenues were up 10% y-o-y, with trading revenues 20% higher to a record $11.6 billion. Investment banking revenues rose 28%. Bank of America saw net income jump 17% to $8.6 billion, as revenues rose 7.2%. Citigroup revenues expanded 14% y-o-y, with its equities segment up 39%. Goldman Sachs revenues rose 14% y-o-y to $17.23 billion. Investment banking fees were 48% higher at $2.84 billion. Equities revenues were up 27%, while fixed income, currency, and commodities (FICC) disappointed with a 10% y-o-y decline.

JPMorgan total assets expanded $542 billion y-o-y to a record $4.90 TN. Loans were $148 billion, or 10.9%, higher y-o-y. Goldman Sachs total assets surged $253 billion, or 14% annualized, during Q1 to a record $2.062 TN. Total assets expanded $296 billion, or 16.7%, y-o-y. Morgan Stanley total assets expanded 11.4% q-o-q to $1.581 TN, with y-o-y growth of $228 billion, or 17.5%.

Subtle perhaps, but bank stocks notably lagged the general market this week despite big quarters. Plenty to worry about.

April 17 – Bloomberg (Yizhu Wang): “The biggest US banks spent the past week tallying more than $185 billion of combined exposure to private credit, an asset class that’s come under pressure in recent months. Executives — many of whom sought to calm investors’ jitters — still see potential in the market. Citizens Financial Group Inc.’s private-credit portfolio will likely climb about 5% in 2026, in line with past years, according to Chief Executive Officer Bruce Van Saun. ‘It’s an area that we feel is important to some of our big relationships,’ Van Saun said. And the bank’s goal is to ‘cement that relationship and do a lot of other business’.”

April 16 – Bloomberg (Laura Benitez, Ellen DiMauro, Hannah Levitt, Olivia Fishlow, and Weihua Li): “For years, Wall Street banks eagerly helped private credit funds amplify their investing firepower with hundreds of billions of dollars in loans, helping them notch ever-higher returns. Now, those same banks are tightening their arrangements, adding to the pressure on managers already reeling from an exodus of investors. Some big banks are raising interest rates for the leverage they provide, and they’re also marking down specific loans posted as collateral. Behind the scenes, that’s prompting private credit fund managers to swap out holdings from the pools as banks including JPMorgan…, Goldman Sachs… and Barclays Plc exercise their right to write down individual assets… The biggest US banks revealed this week that they’ve amassed roughly $180 billion of exposure to private credit firms. JPMorgan pegged its portfolio at $50 billion…”

April 16 – Wall Street Journal (Telis Demos): “Washington has been trying to free up more of the resources of big banks. Banks have wasted no time putting them to work, but on Wall Street rather than Main Street. Big banks’ lending to consumers and midsize companies is moving modestly ahead. But demand for financing has boomed in their Wall Street trading businesses, which serve such clients as private-credit funds, hedge funds and institutional investors. This kind of lending can drive big profits, in part thanks to advantageous regulatory treatment and because it brings in lots of fees for arranging trades, doing deals and other services. But investors are starting to ask how much further banks can increase their Wall Street revenue—especially with growing risks in the current market, ranging from war to artificial-intelligence disruption.”

April 14 – Bloomberg (Liza Tetley and Laura Noonan): “The growing dominance of a handful of banks supplying billions of dollars to help juice bets at hedge funds and proprietary trading firms is sparking new financial stability risks, ratings agency S&P Global Inc. warned. Its latest analysis shows disclosed revenues relating to ‘markets financing’ at four major investment banks — BNP Paribas SA, Barclays Plc, Goldman Sachs... and Morgan Stanley — jumped 25% between 2024 and 2025 to more than $24 billion, representing roughly 30% of these firms’ markets business at that time. Such scale and concentration creates a risk to financial stability, the S&P report warned. Banks’ prime-brokerage units — which includes a range of services to hedge funds and other investment firms — exceeded $2.5 trillion of lending in 2024, a figure that has doubled over the past four years… Hedge funds’ use of borrowed money to fuel their bets — known as leverage — has approached historic highs while assets overseen by those firms hit a record $5 trillion last year.”

April 15 – Financial Times (Ortenca Aliaj and Jill R Shah): “Banks’ soaring exposure to hedge funds and trading firms has created ‘an inherent fragility’ in financial markets, with record leverage and the scale of financing advanced by a handful of big lenders adding to risks, according to a report. Trading firms such as Jane Street, Hudson River Trading and Citadel Securities have reshaped financial markets, with their growth fuelled by lending from traditional investment banks that have retreated from making proprietary bets. Banks’ gross exposure to hedge funds and trading firms was in the trillions, meaning tail risks, which have a low likelihood of occurring but can have a significant impact if they do, were ‘high’, a report from S&P Global Ratings said… ‘Together with record leverage and scale and the concentration of such exposures in a handful of banks, this means the ecosystem exhibits an inherent fragility that could be tested under severe stress,’ it added.”

Despite prospects for peace and stability breaking out all over the world, a few (Torsten Slok and Hank Paulson) were out this week raining on the parade.

April 17 – Bloomberg (Ye Xie): “A buildup of leveraged hedge fund bets in Treasuries has left investors exposed to abrupt position shifts that could amplify stress across global bond markets, according to Apollo Global Management Chief Economist Torsten Slok. Hedge funds now own roughly 8% of the entire $31 trillion US Treasury market, according to Apollo calculations… That’s up from just 3% five years ago. The buildup has been fueled by heavy borrowing, with combined financing via repurchase agreements and prime brokerages now exceeding $6 trillion… ‘Any forced unwind of these leveraged positions could send shockwaves through global fixed income markets,’ he wrote.”

April 16 – Bloomberg (Chris Anstey): “Former Treasury Secretary Henry Paulson called on US authorities to prepare a back-up plan in order to avert a potential collapse in demand for Treasuries — an event that he warned would have ‘vicious’ effects… Paulson said a breakdown in the $31 trillion market for US government debt would be different from the financial crisis he dealt with while at the Treasury’s helm two decades ago. ‘As bad as it was,’ the government had fiscal firepower to address the credit meltdown…”

Hank Paulson with Wall Street Week’s David Westin: “This crisis is different. When you hit the wall and you try to issue Treasuries and the Fed is the only buyer and the prices of Treasuries are going down – interest rates are up – that’s a dangerous thing. So, the thing I am talking about now is – people say, ‘when are you going to hit the wall?’ I obviously don’t know – it’s impossible to know. But the law of economic gravity – you’re not going to know that. And when we hit it, it will be vicious. So, we have to prepare for that eventuality. And I think we need an emergency break the glass plan, which is targeted and short-term, on the shelf – so it’s ready to go when we hit the wall.”.

Westin: “If there is indeed a global shock to the economy (from the Iran War), you’ve dealt with that before with the financial crisis, at the time when you worked hard to bring the globe together… Right now, the globe seems to be going in all different directions – with the U.S. different from Europe, different from China.”

Paulson: “You’ve put your finger on the real difficulty. The first thing is, there’s much more sovereign debt – not just in the U.S., but in Japan and really all around the world. So, you’ve got more debt. And then you’ve got a diversity of economic performance. At the same time, the U.S. economy is strong, China is weak. There are other strengths and weaknesses. So different macroeconomic policies – different monetary policies around the world. So, it’s going to be very important to try to bring it together. That’s why I think it is so important that the U.S. be talking with China and other major economies - monitoring what’s going on, so they’ve got some basis to coordinate when - if and when - there is a global crisis..”

Westin: “When you took the job as Treasury Secretary, in your book “On the Brink,” you talk about debt – about entitlements, borrowing too much money. You were worried about it. It was part of the reason you took the job was to address that. Look at where we were then and where we are now. There’s so much less fiscal headroom to deal with any of the disruption you talk about.”

Paulson: “Wow, it’s breathtaking. The deficit is one trillion dollars. We’re on a path to have it be $3 trillion by 2035… And so you look at this, clearly – we use the word “unsustainable” a lot – but this is on a path to destroy our economic well-being and our national security, which is rooted in our economic strength. The first rule of holes is to stop digging. And we’re digging bigtime. The good news is we’re a rich country. And so there’s plenty we could do if we begin to act. It’s going to take increased revenues – taxes – and dealing with expenses. I know you can raise the revenues without a big drag on growth if you close preferences and loopholes in the tax code. And you can’t deal with the problem unless you deal with entitlements – social security and primarily healthcare… I’ve worked with Congress before, and Congress doesn’t like to do unpleasant things until there is an immediate crisis.”

Westin: “Are we any closer to having that emergency break the glass plan today than we were in 2007 when you were worried about it?”

Paulson: “I’m not there now, but I doubt it. I doubt it. There are so many things out there to deal with right now. To give credit to policymakers, when you look at wars in Ukraine, in Iran, you look at all the conflict around the world, you look at what’s going on with AI, you look what’s going on with the environment, there’s a lot of stuff going on. But we should not forget the deficit.”

It’s worth noting that money market fund assets sank $176 billion last week, surpassing the previous record drop ($169bn) from the week of September 17, 2008. Institutional money funds accounted for $138 billion of the decline. While the contraction was associated with tax payments, it’s still a huge number.

For the Week:

The S&P 500 surged 4.5% (up 4.1% y-t-d), and the Dow rose 3.2% (up 2.9%). The Utilities fell 1.8% (up 8.1%). The Banks gained 2.4% (up 3.1%), and the Broker/Dealers jumped 7.5% (up 10.2%). The Transports spiked 10.2% higher (up 29.2%). The S&P 400 Midcaps jumped 3.5% (up 10.3%), and the small-cap Russell 2000 surged 5.6% (up 11.9%). The Nasdaq 100 jumped 6.2% (up 5.6%). The semiconductors surged 7.5% (up 34.9%). The Biotechs rose 4.1% (up 2.1%). With bullion up $81, the HUI gold index added 1.2% (up 22.0%).

Three-month Treasury bill rates ended the week at 3.597%. Two-year government yields fell nine bps to 3.71% (up 24bps y-t-d). Five-year T-note yields dropped 10 bps to 3.85% (up 12bps). Ten-year Treasury yields declined seven bps to 4.25% (up 8bps). Long bond yields slipped two bps to 4.89% (up 4bps). Benchmark Fannie Mae MBS yields dropped 13 bps to 5.12% (up 8bps).

Italian 10-year yields sank 17 bps to 3.68% (up 13bps y-t-d). Greek 10-year yields fell 15 bps to 3.65% (up 21bps). Spain's 10-year yields dropped 13 bps to 3.39% (up 10bps). German bund yields fell 10 bps to 2.96% (up 11bps). French yields dropped 13 bps to 3.58% (up 2bps). The French to German 10-year bond spread narrowed three to 62 bps. U.K. 10-year gilt yields declined seven bps to 4.76% (up 28bps). The U.K.’s FTSE equities index added 0.6% (up 7.3% y-t-d).

Japan’s Nikkei 225 Equities Index jumped 2.7% (up 16.2% y-t-d). Japan’s 10-year “JGB” yields slipped a basis point to 2.42% (up 36bps y-t-d). France’s CAC40 rose 2.0% (up 3.4%). The German DAX equities index rallied 3.8% (up 0.9%). Spain’s IBEX 35 equities index increased 1.5% (up 6.8%). Italy’s FTSE MIB index rose 2.6% (up 8.7%). EM equities were mostly higher. Brazil’s Bovespa index dipped 0.8% (up 21.5%), and Mexico’s Bolsa index slipped 0.2% (up 8.5%). South Korea’s Kospi surged 5.7% (up 46.9%). India’s Sensex equities index gained 1.2% (down 7.9%). China’s Shanghai Exchange Index increased 1.6% (up 2.1%). Turkey’s Borsa Istanbul National 100 index jumped 3.7% (up 29.5%).

Federal Reserve credit expanded $11.6 billion last week to $6.650 TN, with a 18-week expansion of $159 billion. Fed credit was down $2.240 TN from the June 22, 2022, peak. Since the September 11, 2019 restart of QE, Fed credit has expanded $2.923 TN, or 78%. Fed credit inflated $3.839 TN, or 137%, since November 7, 2012 (701 weeks). Elsewhere, Fed holdings for foreign owners of Treasury, Agency Debt declined another $6.3 billion last week to $2.991 TN - the low back to October 2010. “Custody holdings” were down $302 billion y-o-y, or 9.2%.

Total money market fund assets (MMFA) sank $176 billion to $7.643 TN. MMFA were up $762 billion, or 11.1%, y-o-y - having ballooned a historic $3.059 TN, or 67%, since October 26, 2022.

Total commercial paper recovered a notable $51.2 billion to $1.413 TN. CP gained $20 billion, or 1.4%, y-o-y.

Freddie Mac 30-year fixed mortgage rates fell seven bps to 6.30% (down 53bps y-o-y). Fifteen-year rates dropped nine bps to 5.65% (down 38bps). Bankrate’s survey of jumbo mortgage borrowing costs had the 30-year fixed rate down four bps to 6.53% (down 43bps).

For the week, the U.S. Dollar Index declined 0.6% to 98.098 (down 0.6% y-t-d). On the upside, the Norwegian krone increased 1.6%, the South Korean won 1.6%, the Australian dollar 1.6%, the Canadian dollar 1.1%, the Swedish krone 1.0%, the Swiss franc 0.9%, the New Zealand dollar 0.8%, the South African rand 0.7%, the Brazilian real 0.6%, the British pound 0.4%, the Japanese yen 0.4%, the euro 0.4%, and the Singapore dollar 0.3%.
